input.draft_text:
Indirect Tax
India has a robust mechanism of taxes that helps the government to fund many developmental projects in the country. These taxes can broadly be categorised as direct and indirect tax. Now, what is indirect tax? Simply put, it’s a tax collected by an intermediary, like a shop or service provider, and passed on to the government. For instance, when you buy a product, the tax is already included in the price you pay. Let’s explore how indirect tax works in India, what are its pros and cons and why it matters.
What is indirect tax?
Indirect tax is tax imposed on an individual or entity, which is ultimately paid by another person. That’s why it is termed as ‘indirect’ tax. Indirect tax is levied on supply of goods and services, unlike direct tax which is levied on the income or profits earned by companies, partnership firms, and individuals etc. The framework of indirect tax in India has constantly been strengthened over the years, culminating so far with the introduction and implementation of Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime since July 2017.
So, how does indirect tax work? Let’s understand it with a simple case at a 12% rate:
This is what happens: Ms. A collects ₹180 as tax from Mr. B and submits it to the government. Mr. B then sells the product at ₹1,800 plus ₹216 tax. Since he has already paid ₹180, he only pays the balance ₹36 to the government.
In the end, the customer pays ₹2,016 for the product, of which ₹216 is tax. This shows how an indirect tax service ensures smooth collection for the government while passing the burden onto the final consumer.
Features of Indirect Tax
Given below are the features of Indirect tax in India:
Simplified tax burden
Indirect tax service creates a smooth process for collection. The buyer pays tax when purchasing a product or service. The shopkeeper or producer collects this amount and deposits it with the government.
Progressive structure
The introduction of common rules across the country has brought simplicity. Buyers and sellers both benefit from this clarity. The cascading effect of tax has been erased with the system being more predictable. Fraud cases have also gone down.
Lower scope for tax evasion
One cannot avoid tax now as the tax price is added directly to the goods and services. Every purchase from a packet of chips to a movie ticket, leaves a record, which makes administration more transparent.
Encourages savings and investments
Indirect taxes can influence how people spend. When certain luxury goods are taxed, many choose to cut back and save that money instead. Over time, this shift can encourage more investments or long-term savings.
Levied at the point of purchase
Indirect tax is included in the price you see while shopping. You pay it at the moment of purchase, whether at a supermarket, cinema, mall, or online store. The seller’s role is to pass on the collected tax to the authorities.
Wider social coverage
Unlike direct tax, which only applies to those with taxable income, indirect tax touches almost everyone. It applies to everyday items and services, so people across various income levels contribute. This ensures a steady flow of revenue for the government.
Various types of indirect taxes in India
In India, there are different types of indirect taxes levied by the government. Let’s take a brief look at the most common ones.
Customs Duty
Customs duty is a kind of tax that is imposed on the goods that are transported internationally, across borders. It is defined and governed by the provisions of the Customs Act, 1962. This Act allows the government to impose customs duty on exports and imports, and also to prohibit export and import of goods on need basis. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs (CBIC) is in charge of all things related to customs duty.
Central Excise Duty
Central excise duty is a kind of indirect tax that is levied on the manufacture of goods in India. Unlike customs duty, which applies to goods crossing international borders, excise duty is an indirect tax that is levied only on domestically manufactured specified goods.
Goods and Services Tax (GST)
GST is an indirect tax levied on the supply of goods and services in India. This is recent tax structure on the block. GST has subsumed erstwhile indirect taxes in the country such as VAT, Service tax, Central Excise duty, Additional duties of Customs, Luxury tax, Entry Tax etc. It is paid by the end user of the goods or services, but it is remitted to the government by the seller of goods or the provider of services.
Securities Transaction Tax (STT)
STT is an indirect tax that the Government of India levies on the purchase and sale of securities listed on stock exchanges in India. The securities that are subject to STT include equity, derivatives, and equity-oriented mutual funds.
Value Added Tax (VAT)
VAT dealers collected it on sales and offset it against the tax already paid upon purchase. As mentioned above, VAT was superseded by GST in July 2017, which now plays the same role but in a more centralised and transparent manner.
Stamp Duty
Stamp duty is an indirect tax levied upon specific legal and financial documents in India. Both the central and state governments impose it. Stamp duty makes a document legally valid and acceptable in court if paid. Regulated by the Indian Stamp Act, 1899, it also serves as a stable source of revenue for states.
Entertainment Tax
Entertainment tax is a state-level indirect tax levied on entertainment activities. It's taxed at the point of sale and is borne by the consumer. Rates were widely different across states prior to GST, and they used to average approximately 30%.
Benefits of Indirect Tax
Here’s how indirect tax can be useful:
Everyone pitches in
Because indirect tax is incorporated into buying, everyone contributes, even those who do not pay income tax. This broadens the tax base.
Impossible to dodge
Taxes are paid at the time of purchase, so evasion is impossible and a consistent stream of revenue will flow to the government.
Easy for all
The consumer doesn't have to calculate or pay extra. The sellers take care of the collection of tax, and the system remains easy.
Controls choices
With increased taxes on high-end or harmful products such as tobacco, their use is cut down, whereas necessities might be taxed at a lower or zero rate. This further helps share taxes more evenly and makes sure the burden is spread among everyone.
Limitations of Indirect Tax
Given below are some restrictions of indirect tax:
Makes goods more expensive
Tax on products makes them more expensive to buy. Even essential items such as food or utilities can end up being costly if indirect taxes are applied. This disturbs the budget especially for poor families.
Not fair on the poor
The same rate of tax is paid by everybody, irrespective of earnings. As lesser earners will have to use more of their resources on necessities, the burden hits them more severely.
No civic feel-good factor
Because the tax is embedded in the price, citizens might not associate their purchase with funding public services.
Fluctuating revenue
Indirect taxes often bring unstable income for the government. When demand falls or the economy slows down, collections drop sharply. This makes it harder for the government to plan budgets and achieve financial targets smoothly.
Indirect Tax vs. Income Tax
Beginners may often confuse indirect tax with income tax. However, both are vastly different in terms of their charging mechanism and who pays them. The table below highlights the key difference between the two types of taxes:
How to use income tax calculator online
Here’s a step-by-step guide to using an Income Tax Calculator online.
Enter your basic details, like age, gender, etc.
Fill in the details of your income, like the gross annual income, etc.
Enter the details of any investments and eligible deductions you can claim. This includes investments u/s 80C, home loans, education loans, savings account interest etc.
Submit the details to see the amount of tax you will be liable for.
Impact of Indirect Taxes on the Economy
Indirect tax services impact the economy in numerous ways, ranging from the choices of consumers to trade policies.
Effect on consumption patterns
When tax is increased on some products such as luxury items, individuals tend to switch to more affordable options or spend less money altogether. This impacts demand in all sectors and assists the government in directing consumption towards more socially or economically beneficial products.
Government revenue generation
Indirect taxes yield consistent revenues as they are levied on daily transactions. GST has simplified compliance and widened the base for taxation. This assured stream of funds enables governments to finance development, welfare schemes, and public expenditure.
Influence on trade and imports
Duties on imports determine the extent to which foreign products are appealing in relation to domestic products. Import duties can shield industries domestically when they are high, and low duties might stimulate foreign trade. This equilibrium determines competitiveness and affects total trade flows.
Key Takeaways
Indirect tax is levied on goods and services at the time of transaction.
It disperses the burden to all consumers and provide consistent revenue.
Compliance is simpler because businesses gather and pay the tax.
Indirect taxes are of different types – custom, stamp, central excise, GST, STT.
Advantages of indirect tax service are broad coverage, convenience, stable funds, and equitable distribution.
Disadvantages include uncertainty of income and increased expenses for consumers.
Indirect tax is different from income tax, which is levied straight on an individual's income.
Indirect tax affects what individuals purchase, how trade occurs, and government revenues.
Conclusion
This is how it sums up the few of the important details about indirect taxes. You’ve also been introduced to how they’re different from income tax. That would help you to understand personal finance and manage your funds accordingly.
FAQs
What are 5 examples of indirect taxes in India?
Indirect taxes in India fall under these 5 categories – customs duty, central excise duty, security transaction tax, goods and service tax, stamp duty.
Is GST an indirect tax?
Yes. GST is a part of indirect tax introduced in India in 2017. It merged varied taxes like VAT, service tax, central sales tax into one creating a single integrated tax system.
Who pays indirect tax?
The final consumer pays indirect tax while buying goods or services. Businesses only collect and pass it on, so the actual burden falls on people making the purchase.
Which indirect tax is still applicable apart from GST?
Apart from GST, customs duty and stamp duty are still levied in India. These taxes apply on imports, exports, and legal documents, and are not part of GST.

output.final_text:
Indirect Tax
India has a robust mechanism of taxes that helps the government to fund many developmental projects in the country. These taxes can broadly be categorised as direct and indirect tax. Now, what is indirect tax? Simply put, it’s a tax collected by an intermediary, like a shop or service provider, and passed on to the government. For instance, when you buy a product, the tax is already included in the price you pay. Let’s explore how indirect tax works in India, what are its pros and cons and why it matters.
What is indirect tax?
Indirect tax is tax imposed on an individual or entity, which is ultimately paid by another person. That’s why it is termed as ‘indirect’ tax. Indirect tax is levied on supply of goods and services, unlike direct tax which is levied on the income or profits earned by companies, partnership firms, and individuals etc. The framework of indirect tax in India has constantly been strengthened over the years, culminating so far with the introduction and implementation of Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime since July 2017.
So, how does indirect tax work? Let’s understand it with a simple case at a 12% rate:
Particulars Supplier (Ms. A) Shop Owner (Mr. B) Base price ₹1,500 ₹1,800 Tax @ 12% ₹180 ₹216 Price with tax ₹1,680 ₹2,106 Tax already paid ₹0 ₹180 Net tax paid to Govt. ₹180 ₹36 This is what happens: Ms. A collects ₹180 as tax from Mr. B and submits it to the government. Mr. B then sells the product at ₹1,800 plus ₹216 tax. Since he has already paid ₹180, he only pays the balance ₹36 to the government.
In the end, the customer pays ₹2,016 for the product, of which ₹216 is tax. This shows how an indirect tax service ensures smooth collection for the government while passing the burden onto the final consumer.
Features of Indirect Tax
Given below are the features of Indirect tax in India:
● Simplified tax burden Indirect tax service creates a smooth process for collection. The buyer pays tax when purchasing a product or service. The shopkeeper or producer collects this amount and deposits it with the government. ● Progressive structure The introduction of common rules across the country has brought simplicity. Buyers and sellers both benefit from this clarity. The cascading effect of tax has been erased with the system being more predictable. ● Lower scope for tax evasion One cannot avoid tax now as the tax price is added directly to the goods and services. Every purchase from a packet of chips to a movie ticket, leaves a record, which makes administration more transparent.
● Encourages savings and investments Indirect taxes can influence how people spend. When certain luxury goods are taxed, many choose to cut back and save that money instead. Over time, the savings can encourage more investments which can grow into long-term savings for your financial goals. ● Levied at the point of purchase Indirect tax is included in the price you see while shopping. You pay it at the moment of purchase, whether at a supermarket, cinema, mall, or online store. The seller’s role is to pass on the collected tax to the authorities. ● Wider social coverage Unlike direct tax, which only applies to those with taxable income, indirect tax is usually applicable to almost everyone who consumes goods and services. It applies to everyday items and services, so people across various income levels contribute. This ensures a steady flow of revenue for the government.
Various types of indirect taxes in India
In India, there are different types of indirect taxes levied by the government. Let’s take a brief look at the most common ones.
1. Customs Duty
Customs duty is a kind of tax that is imposed on the goods that are transported internationally, across borders. It is defined and governed by the provisions of the Customs Act, 1962. This Act allows the government to impose customs duty on exports and imports, and also to prohibit export and import of goods on need basis. The Central Board of Indirect Taxes & Customs (CBIC) is in charge of all things related to customs duty. 2. Central Excise Duty
Central excise duty is a kind of indirect tax that is levied on the manufacture of goods in India. Unlike customs duty, which applies to goods crossing international borders, excise duty is an indirect tax that is levied only on domestically manufactured specified goods. 3. Goods and Services Tax (GST)
GST is an indirect tax levied on the supply of goods and services in India. This is recent tax structure on the block. GST has subsumed erstwhile indirect taxes in the country such as VAT, Service tax, Central Excise duty, Additional duties of Customs, Luxury tax, Entry Tax etc. It is paid by the end user of the goods or services, but it is remitted to the government by the seller of goods or the provider of services. 4. Securities Transaction Tax (STT)
STT is an indirect tax that the Government of India levies on the purchase and sale of securities listed on stock exchanges in India. The securities that are subject to STT include equity, derivatives, and equity-oriented mutual funds. 5. Value Added Tax (VAT)
VAT dealers collected it on sales and offset it against the tax already paid upon purchase. As mentioned above, VAT was superseded by GST in July 2017, which now plays the same role but in a more centralised and transparent manner. 6. Stamp Duty
Stamp duty is an indirect tax levied upon specific legal and financial documents in India. Stamp duties are levied by the Central Government, but within the States are collected and appropriated by the concerned States. Stamp duty makes a document legally valid and
acceptable in court if paid. Regulated by the Indian Stamp Act, 1899, it also serves as a stable source of revenue for states. 7. Entertainment Tax
Entertainment tax is a state-level indirect tax levied on entertainment activities. It's taxed at the point of sale and is borne by the consumer. Rates were widely different across states prior to GST, and they used to range between 15% and 110%.
Benefits of Indirect Tax
Here’s how indirect tax can be useful:
● Everyone pitches in Because indirect tax is incorporated into buying, almost everyone pays this tax when buying goods and services, even those who do not pay income tax. This broadens the tax base. ● Impossible to dodge Taxes are paid at the time of purchase, so evasion is impossible and a consistent stream of revenue will flow to the government. ● Easy for all The consumer doesn't have to calculate or pay extra. The sellers take care of the collection of tax, and the system remains easy. ● Effect on lifestyle choices With increased taxes on high-end or harmful products such as tobacco, their use is cut down, whereas necessities might be taxed at a lower or zero rate. This further helps share taxes more evenly and makes sure the burden is spread among everyone.
Limitations of Indirect Tax
Given below are some restrictions of indirect tax:
● Increases the cost of goods Indirect taxes increase the cost of products. This can affect the budget of households and might increase the overall outgo. ● Affects those with low incomes The same rate of tax is paid by everybody, irrespective of earnings. As lesser earners will have to use more of their resources on necessities, the burden hits them more severely. ● Fluctuating revenue Though indirect taxes give revenue to the government, the revenue is not stable. When demand falls or the economy slows down, collections drop sharply. This makes it harder for the government to plan budgets and achieve financial targets smoothly.
Indirect Tax vs. Income Tax
Beginners may often confuse indirect tax with income tax. However, both are vastly different in terms of their charging mechanism and who pays them. The table below highlights the key difference between the two types of taxes:
Point of Difference Income Tax Indirect Tax Nature A type of direct tax levied on income generated by individuals or organisations
A tax on services and goods, levied at every stage of supply or sale
Governing law Income Tax Act, 1961 Multiple indirect tax legislation, now predominantly enacted under GST Who pays Paid directly to the government by the taxpayer as per their income slab
Collected from sellers but finally paid by consumers
Timing Computed annually on income, submitted according to deadlines
Paid immediately at point of purchase or transaction Burden shift Cannot be shifted; it's paid by the taxpayer
Shifted from seller to end consumer through pricing
How to use income tax calculator online
Here’s a step-by-step guide to using an Income Tax Calculator online.
● Enter your basic details, like age, gender, etc.
● Fill in the details of your income, like the gross annual income, etc.
● Enter the details of any investments and eligible deductions you can claim. This includes investments u/s 80C, home loans, education loans, savings account interest etc.
● Submit the details to see the amount of tax you will be liable for.
Impact of Indirect Taxes on the Economy
Indirect tax services impact the economy in numerous ways, ranging from the choices of consumers to trade policies.
● Effect on consumption patterns When tax is increased on some products such as luxury items, individuals tend to switch to more affordable options or spend less money altogether. This impacts demand in all sectors and assists the government in directing consumption towards more socially or economically beneficial products. ● Government revenue generation Indirect taxes yield consistent revenues as they are levied on daily transactions. GST has simplified compliance and widened the base for taxation. This assured stream of funds enables governments to finance development, welfare schemes, and public expenditure. ● Influence on trade and imports Duties on imports determine the extent to which foreign products are appealing in relation to domestic products. Import duties can shield industries domestically when they are high, and low duties might stimulate foreign trade. This equilibrium determines competitiveness and affects total trade flows.
Key Takeaways
● Indirect tax is levied on goods and services at the time of transaction. ● It disperses the burden to all consumers and provide consistent revenue. ● Compliance is simpler because businesses levy the tax, collect it from the consumers and pay it to the government. ● Indirect taxes are of different types – custom, stamp, central excise, GST, STT.
● Advantages of indirect tax service are broad coverage, convenience, stable funds, and equitable distribution. ● Disadvantages include uncertainty of income and increased expenses for consumers. ● Indirect tax is different from income tax, which is levied straight on an individual's income. ● Indirect tax affects what individuals purchase, how trade occurs, and government revenues.
Conclusion
This is how it sums up the few of the important details about indirect taxes. You’ve also been introduced to how they’re different from income tax. That would help you to understand personal finance and manage your funds accordingly.
FAQs
What are 5 examples of indirect taxes in India?
Indirect taxes in India fall under these 5 categories – customs duty, central excise duty, security transaction tax, goods and service tax, stamp duty.
Is GST an indirect tax?
Yes. GST is a part of indirect tax introduced in India in 2017. It merged varied taxes like VAT, service tax, central sales tax into one creating a single integrated tax system.
Who pays indirect tax?
The final consumer pays indirect tax while buying goods or services. Businesses only collect and pass it on, so the actual burden falls on people making the purchase.
Which indirect tax is still applicable apart from GST?
Apart from GST, customs duty and stamp duty are still levied in India. These taxes apply on imports, exports, and legal documents, and are not part of GST.
